w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: This auth system is made of two connected layers:
— decides which personal or business account the user enters.
choose an existing account or create a new one,
enter the correct workspace based on account type.
This documentation is now aligned to the current codebase. The items below reflect what is implemented today versus what is still planned.
Implemented now: phone number + password authentication on the entry auth screen.
Implemented now: language selection through the dropdown.
Implemented now: signup currently collects full name, phone number, and password.
This screen contains all onboarding and sign-in entry points.
Language quick selection row (planned, not currently visible)
Forgot Password action (planned; currently hidden because the route handler is not wired)
Bottom switch text: “Don’t have an account? Sign Up”
This screen is part of the intended post-authentication flow, but the current implementation is still a placeholder and does not yet provide working account selection or account creation actions.
Choose or create an account to get started
This modal is used to create a personal finance workspace.
This modal is used to create a business workspace.
This system does more than simple login and signup.
Authentication currently proves the user’s identity through:
After authentication, the system asks which workspace the user wants to use:
Personal account for personal finance tracking,
Business account for POS, inventory, billing, and business operations,
Existing linked accounts for returning users.
This design allows one identity to manage multiple workspaces.
User selects a language if needed.
User stays on the Login tab.
User enters phone number and password.
On success, the app creates the session and prepares the next implemented onboarding or account step.
account selection, linked accounts can be fetched and shown there.
User continues into the next available screen.
The app stores active auth/session context.
User enters the app dashboard or next onboarding step.
User is routed to the next implemented onboarding step.
If account setup is enabled later, the user can create personal or business accounts there.
User enters the app when onboarding is complete.
User taps Forgot Password. (Planned flow; current auth screen does not yet expose this action.)
User enters email or is sent to reset flow.
Backend sends reset link or OTP.
User signs in with the new password.
App sets it as active account.
App sets it as active account.
Change app language and persist it.
Purpose: Authenticate an existing user with phone number and password.
fetch linked accounts when that flow is implemented.
optional linked account list when account selection is implemented
Purpose: Create a new user identity from the current signup form.
route to the next implemented onboarding step or placeholder account screen.
Purpose: Start password reset once the auth entry screen exposes the action.
phoneNumber or email (final product decision still pending)
Retrieve accounts linked to the current user.
phone number must not be empty,
phone number must be valid format,
phone number should be trimmed and normalized before submit.
Phone Number: valid format and country-aware
Phone Number is optional but validated if entered
City / Location is optional unless product decides otherwise
Phone Number is optional but validated if entered
PAN / Tax Registration may be optional during onboarding
Business Address may be optional at onboarding
must match one of the supported options
if “Other” is selected, product may allow extra detail later
default can be NPR if the product is Nepal-first
Please enter a valid phone number.
Something went wrong. Please try again.
Phone number already in use. Please log in instead.
Password must be at least 8 characters.
Please enter a valid phone number.
If an account exists for the provided identifier, reset instructions have been sent.
do not block the app permanently without action.
chip and dropdown stay in sync.
User can enter phone number and password.
Invalid phone number shows validation error.
Valid credentials log the user in.
Invalid credentials show a clear error.
Forgot Password action is planned but currently hidden until the route passes the handler.
Google and Apple login options are planned but currently not visible.
User can switch between Login and Sign Up.
Current signup fields are full name, phone number, and password.
Successful signup creates a valid session.
Existing phone number cannot be registered twice.
User is routed to the next implemented onboarding step after signup.
User can authenticate with Google once the option is implemented.
User can authenticate with Apple once the option is implemented.
Canceling social auth should return safely to the auth screen.
Successful social auth should create a valid session.
If no account exists, user should be routed to account creation or account setup.
User can switch language from the dropdown.
Quick language chips are planned but not currently visible.
Selected language is visibly reflected in the dropdown state.
Account selection is documented, but the current screen is still a placeholder.
User cannot yet open an existing account from the placeholder screen.
User cannot yet create Personal Account from this screen.
User cannot yet create Business Account from this screen.
When implemented, account creation options should remain available if no accounts exist.
When implemented, the selected account should become the active account.
Account is created on valid submission.
User continues into the app after success.
Business Type dropdown shows all supported options.
Currency dropdown shows all supported currencies.
Business account is created on valid submission.
User continues into the business workspace after success.
The current codebase has a simpler signup contract than the earlier documentation, and a few product decisions still need to be finalized.
Signup currently collects full name, phone number, and password only.
when account selection should become active after authentication,
when Forgot Password should be exposed in UI,
when Google and Apple auth should be exposed in UI,
whether quick language chips should ship in addition to the dropdown.
creates or accesses the user identity.
chooses which finance workspace to enter.
is for business operations like POS, billing, and inventory.
finishes workspace creation and enters the app.
For this auth system, WatermelonDB should store the
local app state and local workspace metadata
, not the raw backend authentication tokens.
14.1 What should be stored in WatermelonDB
14.2 What should not be stored in WatermelonDB
These should go into secure storage such as:
Encrypted Shared Preferences / Keystore on Android
14.3 Recommended local tables for this auth flow
For the current auth and account flow, the local database can be designed around these tables:
(optional now, but strongly recommended if one user may access more than one business or if future team access is planned)
Purpose: - store app-level local settings - store selected language - store onboarding state - store currently active account id - store currently active user id
Purpose: - cache the signed-in user profile locally - allow the app to restore user context quickly
Purpose: - store all personal and business accounts linked to the current user - drive the account selection screen
Purpose: - store business-only details for accounts where
Purpose: - manage which users belong to which account - define user access and role inside the account - support future multi-user business access - support roles like owner, admin, staff, accountant
Below is a clean auth/account schema structure aligned with the current app flow.
one local user can have many accounts
one account can be personal or business
one business profile belongs to one business account
one account can have many memberships
one user can have many memberships
Store locally: - user profile snapshot in
Do not store tokens in WatermelonDB.

Recommended behavior: - clear local account records tied to the signed-out session if the app is single-user - clear active account id - clear active user id - keep selected language if it is app-wide - wipe secure tokens from secure storage
- reload workspace-specific data after switch - keep user profile unchanged

, the structure should not group everything under one giant auth module. Instead, keep each business concern in its own feature and let the route layer compose them. This matches your rulebook: routes handle navigation and handoff, factories only wire dependencies, feature types stay inside the feature, and Watermelon model/schema stay in the data layer. fileciteturn1file2 fileciteturn1file3
21.2 Why this split is better for your architecture
= account list, account switching, memberships, active account state -
This avoids mixing: - login logic, - account selection logic, - personal profile creation, - business profile creation, - app settings.
That separation fits your rulebook’s feature ownership model and keeps each feature small and maintainable. fileciteturn1file0 fileciteturn1file4
Auth should only handle identity-level actions: - login - signup - forgot password - Google login - Apple login - session restore handoff
This feature owns account selection and active account context, not login.
This feature owns creation and update of personal account details.
This should be a separate feature because it has its own form, schema, model, validation, dropdowns, and likely future settings.
Since language is a real product concern in your screens, keeping it in its own feature is cleaner than hiding it inside auth.

Keep Watermelon files in the owning feature when the table belongs only to that feature. That follows your rulebook: Watermelon model/schema belong in the data layer, and feature-level types stay in the feature. fileciteturn1file4
Do not over-split too early. Start with this practical version:
That is the best balance for your current product.
business type and currency-driven form logic
This is more correct than putting everything under
, because after login the flows are no longer auth-only — they become account, profile setup, and user access flows. fileciteturn1file0 fileciteturn1file1
For this auth system, the best architecture is:
for tokens and sensitive auth secrets
to convert between backend DTOs, local Watermelon models, and domain entities
This keeps the auth system secure, scalable, and aligned with the existing eLekha architecture.
Next recommended additions for this auth module are:
repository layer for auth/account local persistence
sync strategy between backend and WatermelonDB
